data.title: Ducommun: Extreme Undervaluation Offers Strong Buy Opportunity
data.description:
Ducommun (NYSE:DCO) reported its fourth quarter and full-year earnings on the 27th of February. The company missed estimates on EPS but beat on revenues. This seemed enough to send the stock prices down by more than 6%.

In November, I covered Ducommun and attached a buy rating to the name. The stock, however, lost 14.6% since then underperforming the 2.2% decline for the S&P 500. While this is performance is underwhelming, I do note that since I initiated coverage in 2023 the stock is still showing a market outperforming return.

In this report, I will discuss the most recent earnings and the outlook for 2025. Additionally, I will update my stock price target and rating for Ducommun.

Ducommun Growth Was Not Impressive In 2024

Fourth quarter sales grew 2.6% consistent with the growth rate observed in the third quarter. Gross margins increased 1.8 points to 23.5% with adjusted operating income growing 1.7% to $16.1 million.

The investment case for Ducommun largely hinges on the growth trends in commercial aerospace as well as defense. However, the fourth quarter results do not really reflect the growth trends. Additionally, with doubts on defense spendings, the strength in defense growth observed previously could work against the company. The reason is that investors may assume that part of the growth will fade, and that could put a dent in the company’s growth profile.

The Structural Systems segment saw its revenues increase by 5.6% driven by the Airbus A220 (OTCPK:EADSF) and Sikorsky S-92 (LMT) programs. Adjusted operating income, however, declined 33.6% to $8.3 million. This was driven by an unfavorable mix. Part of the soft growth and operating profit was driven by the work stoppage at Boeing (BA).

The Electronics Systems segment saw flat sales at $107 million. Operating income, however, rose sharply. A 74% increase in operating income to $19 million brought the margins to 17.7%. The increase was driven by positive mix and cost rationalization. Ducommun has been consolidating production into fewer facilities, and it is focusing more on engineered products sales. The benefits, which still have to fully materialize, are already visible in the results.

Upward Pressure In 2025 For Ducommun

Ducommun provided the slide above as the full-year outlook. To me, that is just a lousy way to provide an outlook. The company is aiming for mid-single digit growth. While I am not necessarily doubting the company’s ability to realize its Vision 2027 target to reach $950 million to $1 billion in revenues, it does point at the growth rate having to increase from mid-single digits in 2025 to high single digits to double digits in 2026 and 2027.

Part of the growth has to come from the recovery at Boeing, which should be better visible in the second half of the year. Additionally, Airbus is also working towards higher build rates, which should support revenue growth. While I am not necessarily expecting a significant slowdown in military sales, I do view the current defense budget environment as a potential risk.

So, overall the long-term drivers are there, but budget risk for defense as well as timing risk for build rates of commercial jets has increased.

Ducommun Stock Is A Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I believe that the stock price decline has opened up a strong buy opportunity. While EBITDA and free cash flow were below expectations, the company should still be able to grow significantly in the years ahead. EBITDA growth towards 2026 has been revised downward from 14.6% to 13.9%. Similarly, the free cash flow growth rate has been revised downward from 98.3% to 90.9%.

Given the stock price decline and the expected growth, I am upgrading the stock to strong buy with a $91.05 price target. This represents nearly 60% upside, and we note that the company is currently undervalued against its 2024 earnings.

Conclusion: Ducommun Stock Is Undervalued

The fourth quarter results did show that market pressures are affecting the company’s growth and profitability, and that remains the major risk item. Timing of production rate increases also determine the growth for Ducommun.

While that growth has been below expectations in 2024, the company remains positioned extremely well within the market to benefit from growth in commercial airplane production.

The company will need to see accelerated double-digit growth in 2026-2027 to reach its Vision 2027 targets. So, it is definitely not a walk in the park for Ducommun. What also makes the stock a bit less attractive is that it does not pay a dividend. So, there is no strong financial incentive for investors to take a “buy and wait” approach for strong growth that is expected in commercial airplane production.

However, the company should have a growing cash pile, which could see another uptick from the sale of facilities that have been closed this year. So, there is an opportunity for Ducommun to eventually pay a dividend or lower its debt.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MasterBrand Cabinets: Macro Uncertainty Top Of Mind
data.description:
Introduction

MasterBrand Cabinets (NYSE:MBC) posted a contextually okay quarter, but with sales continuing to trend in the wrong direction thanks to “unexpected” softness, shares understandably traded down post-earnings and are now in the mid-$14/share range. To this end too, the biggest valuation question is macro, and just how much this can impact near-term economics, i.e., if one believes that we’re reaching a point of stability today, the go-forward IRR here is decently attractive.

Sales: Macro’s The Question

Q4 sales came in at $668M, down just over 1% from $677M posted in the prior year Q4, which thus puts their FY24 sales down 1%. Broken out by channel, we can see, however, that it wasn’t evenly spread across the board. Their Dealer sales and their Builder sales both grew year-over-year, with their Dealer sales growing 2.1% to ~$360M and their Builder channel growing 1.7% to ~$85M. Their Retail channel, however, declined nearly 8% to ~$222M.

As I previously pointed out, these are not organic trends. They acquired Supreme in July 2024, which added ~$60M in quarterly sales, and specifically, D/D sales. Indeed, Q4 organic sales were “driven by volume declines of 6% and lower net average selling price (ASP) of 4%,” but “offset by 9% growth from our Supreme acquisition.” That would then translate to ~$61M in sales contribution from Supreme, offsetting a ~10% organic sales decline year-over-year.

These incremental data points here are still largely supportive of my structural thesis on MBC, but the magnitude was a bit of a surprise. Per the call, this wasn’t much more than unexpected end-market demand declines for each channel. The previously announced price increases didn’t flow through to the extent they expected here in Q4. More specifically, the price increases were pushed through their Dealer channel. However, they went largely unrealized thanks to a “miss to our volume expectations in this portion of the market.” So, when I think about listed price year-over-year, it still might’ve been a marginal tailwind, but broadly speaking, we can say it was likely around flat.

Similar to Q3 though and similar to what’s hurting AMWD, they’re feeling the effects from trade-downs that’s thus hurting ASPs, particularly on the Builder side but now on the R&R side. They called out that “[o]ur made-to-order offering, which is a higher price point product, was disproportionately impacted by the volume declines compared to the rest of our product portfolio, causing a mix shift we had not seen earlier in the year,” and that “[t]his negative mix was the primary driver of the 4% year-over-year net ASP decline in our legacy business during the quarter.” Indeed, given what’s happening to prices organically, this would then be consistent with that conclusion.

As for the 6% volume decline, this is larger than the flat volume trend in Q3 and something similar in Q2 with sales only down 3% then. In other words, like I called out earlier, demand did indeed decline further in the quarter, which we can further confirm by looking at Q4’s sequential trend reflecting a larger-than-normal seasonal decline.

While unexpected, I continue to believe these are macro declines. You can read the broader industry data points referenced in my prior writeup around Home Depot (HD), Lowe’s (LOW), and competitor American Woodmark (AMWD) which show that the whole industry is seeing declines, not just MBC. The sequential data points support this too - since the end of Q3, completions have continued to decline through the end of 2024, although flattening out to start 2025, and the same goes for R&R spend with a slight increase in Q4. However, both are still about flat-to-down year-over-year, which would be consistent with MBC’s demand trends. This hypothesis is also supported by the mix trends that we’ve been seeing in the quarter too. That is, with both AMWD and MBC seeing mix shifts to lower ASP products, this would support the idea of general macro softness as consumers search for lower-cost products to save money.

So, I’m not inclined to attribute these declines to share losses, although I should note that relative to AMWD’s Q3 year-over-year channel-specific results, which were down ~5%, MBC is now underperforming. I can’t explain this yet, but my bet, given MBC’s history, continues to be that they’re not losing share and that this is just market trends. If we zoom out to a full-year basis, MBC has been winning share, particularly on the Builder side via new product intros.

From MBC’s angle, there hasn’t been any shift in their value proposition. In fact, MBC is actually making some decent improvements in their value proposition through various “tech” investments - digitizing plant operations - with a notable consumer-facing one being the addition of “MasterBrand Connect”, a newly introduced ordering portal. And on top of this too, they’ve apparently seen material quality improvements related to these operational investments as they’ve “...heard from customers that our performance on both quality and delivery is industry-leading.”

However, price is a very sensitive demand lever, and both Retail and Dealer can see more promotional activity that can shift share around. To this end, on the Q3 call, they talked about promotional intensity being relatively stable, noting “[t]rade downs and promotional activity are anticipated to continue at a similar pace but we expect the impact to moderate from a year-over-year perspective as we continue to lap the onset of these in 2023.” But then on the Q4 call, they noted that it’s “...definitely more competitive in the opening price point side of the business.” I don’t necessarily infer growing price competition, however, particularly as AMWD is also pushing through price increases.

With ~$668M in Q4 sales, MBC is running at an annualized rate of around $2.75B per historical seasonality. Going forward, pricing hasn’t fully been realized, so as inventory filters through, that’s probably going to be a low-single-digit benefit to their D/D channel specifically, or maybe 100-ish bps to MBC on a consolidated basis.

This estimate doesn’t contemplate price increases through the Builder or Retailer channel, but to this end, they noted on the call that while “...other channels have taken longer to implement price, particularly in the lower price point products,” they “...have made some progress here since December.” In other words, Dave has sort of admitted to MBC implementing price increases in their other channels too. Thus, perhaps we’ll be looking at a 1-2% consolidated price benefit over the next 12 months. But that seems like an okay estimate long-term too.

Now, will mix continue to be a near-term headwind and offset this? Probably, but unlikely as a 400 bps headwind. Mix trends are tied to the macro for which this variable will eventually abate, although we don’t necessarily know when. I’m not going to overweight Q4 results and management isn’t either as they point to early 2025 data and the respective sequential trends as something that’s more reflective of general stability than continued demand degradation. At some point though, both the mix shift and the macro-driven volume declines should simultaneously go away.

As for the tariffs impact, they provided helpful context, noting that finished goods from Mexico and Canada represented roughly 10% of sales with only a low-single-digit percentage of goods sourced from China. So, this doesn’t overly expose them, but it will nonetheless have an impact on their costs. As I’ve discussed before, based on what we saw with tariff increases back in 2018, I think a low-to-mid-single-digit sales impact is reasonable - I was modeling a 3% sales (net of price) headwind and I’m okay keeping it there.

Zooming out, as these headwinds fade, they should pick up additional cross-sell wins via Supreme. This is more of a FY25 onward tailwind as they’re still in the process of merely getting the organizations organized and integrated. And as management has pointed out, there’s some training that needs to happen too, so the benefit timing isn’t immediate and they’ve also had the business for half a year now. Over time, I wrote that 5-10% of their Dealer sales wasn’t a bad estimate - i.e., $70M in added annual Dealer sales. There was only a 20% Dealer overlap between them, so that leaves them a good deal of runway.

Beyond this though, I’m merely expecting them to track market volumes over time - I don’t see where they’re going to really be able to scale the organization geographically. So, for FY25, we’re looking at price increases and marginal cross-selling benefits offset by likely continued macro deterioration resulting in mix and volume declines on top of tariff headwinds. My initial conclusion was to model mid-single-digit sales declines and I continue to believe that’s probably an okay estimate. However, one should note that management is modeling “[o]rganic net sales flat” with a mid-single-digit increase in consolidated sales (which is inorganically boosted by Supreme’s annualization). Thus, either (1) I’m understating the price benefit, (2) they’re expecting better macro conditions, and/or (3) cross-selling will be more materially favorable than I expect.

We’ll see what happens. My belief more broadly though is that once we get past macro-driven headwinds - which I admit could continue longer than I’m anticipating - that this is a business that can low-single-digits over time with the market. So, I’m modeling a 7% decline off of that $2.75B figure stated earlier to give myself a little more cushion, and then low-single-digit thereafter starting in FY26, equaling FY28 sales of ~$2.754B.

Margins: Mix of Impacts

From a margin perspective, they posted gross margins of 30.4%, down 250 bps from the 32.9% posted in the prior year. This was offset, however, by some opex leverage as EBITDA margins came in at 11.2%, which was down only 150 bps from the 12.7% posted in Q4 last year, implying 100 bps of opex leverage.

The gross margins aren’t hard to explain because they essentially lay it out for us. The price increases they implemented have yet to be realized in earnest, and this is causing some degradation because “...inflation has not subsided…” - i.e., higher wages, material, and freight costs. The net result “...caused us to remain in a negative price/cost relationship during the fourth quarter,” as essentially, cost inflation is recognized immediately while price lags. In terms of magnitude, they don’t break it out, but it certainly could’ve been in the 100-200 bps range.

Product mix shift to lower ASPs isn’t something I’m contextualizing as a material impact. It’s possible that certain products have better/worse margins, but I don’t think their made-to-order products were all that different from the rest of the business. Semi-customer, per AMWD, tends to be higher margin, but all considered, gross margins are probably roughly similar. By channel though, this was likely a negative shift as Builder margins tend to be the worst behind Retail which tends to be slightly worse than Dealer - a competitive and bargaining power dynamic. So, with Builder holding up better (organically speaking), that was probably a slight headwind.

With respect to Supreme’s mix impact, we know that since the addition of the acquisition, they’ve added about $120M in total sales - ~$60M in both periods. And per the 10-K, they incurred ~$84M in COGS, which thus amounts to a gross margin for Supreme of 30%, slightly lower than gross margins for MBC legacy business. Thus, we’re probably looking at something like a 20-30-40 bps gross margin headwind from Supreme’s addition.

Then finally, gross margins are also being negatively impacted by the decline in organic sales. We’ve already covered the price and mix changes above, but with volumes down 6%, that naturally results in fixed cost deleverage, which they admit, noting that the “...the unusually rapid decline this year impacted our ability to flex manufacturing quickly enough to preserve markets.” Indeed, while half of COGS are material, the other half being a mix of labor and overhead, which can’t be easily flexed. So, you put all of these impacts together and that gives us the margin decline.

Moving down to the operating line, there’s evidently some offsetting opex leverage, which is surprising, obviously, considering the sales decline. From a reported perspective, minus Supreme, SG&A went from ~$570M in FY23 to about ~$550M in FY24, or ~$150M to ~$130M in Q4. To emphasize, this is reported SG&A and we’re looking at adjusted EBITDA margins, but it still goes to show the general opex trends/leverage. This is notable too considering they’ve been making investments throughout 2024 which are being expensed in SG&A.

However, they’ve continued to benefit from restructuring cost savings, which is a mix of both facility consolidations and headcount reduction. Also, in last year’s Q4, they made the “...decision to permanently close our Newton, Kansas manufacturing facility, which had previously been idled.” So, there were probably some marginal carry over savings from this. All of this would be consistent though with their attribution of “...higher-than-anticipated continuous improvement savings and lower variable compensation” as EBITDA cost tailwinds year-over-year.

Then finally, the other EBITDA benefit is seemingly mix via Supreme. As I wrote last time, “Supreme posted ~$58M for the 12 months ended March 2024 for which, while we don’t have the TTM sales for that period, reflects EBITDA margins of ~23% on their FY23 sales of $253M.” Thus, this is higher than the low-double-digit margins posted by MBC’s legacy business, so we can probably infer a 40-50 bps benefit here year-over-year.

Looking ahead, a few things should happen. Holding volumes constant, I would expect their gross margins to improve as we go forward with price increases starting to flow through which’ll help better match price with the recent cost inflation. The cost catch though will be tariffs like we discussed earlier, and I can imagine that there might be some lag here between getting prices pass through and when costs are recognized, just as we’re seeing today. In the end though, my conclusion that they can structurally earn low-30% gross margins doesn’t really change.

I’m not overly worried about mix because I don’t find there to be any structural movements happening, and with respect to fixed cost leverage, this should be about a wash too from a sales perspective. From a cost perspective though, their intention to extract more savings beyond today should be a net tailwind. Per the call, they’re anticipating another $50M in incremental savings in 2025, which is on top of $50M achieved in 2024.

This should result in a net decrease again in costs holding aside their changes in sales. More specifically, in Q4 they’ve just consolidated 3 plants in North Carolina into 1 plant, so that should save on lease expense. Coupling this with what sounds to be further headcount reduction opportunities, they will capture some incremental savings. Now, those headcount savings likely include the opportunities with Supreme for which I’ll remind you that they were targeting $28M in cost synergies by FY27-FY28 between procurement, plant, and headcount savings.

The only offset is their intention to invest an “incremental $15M” into their Tech Enabled Initiative, which is on top of $20M spent in 2024. Per management’s comments, these costs may not be recurring - i.e., as they eventually slow their tech investments - as some of this is seemingly “catch-up” related - SG&A should capture some slight tailwind. I’m not modeling this - i.e., I’m assuming that these are recurring costs and/or they have to maintain this level of spend for competitive reasons - but I wouldn’t be surprised if it turns into a slight benefit post-FY25.

Putting these two together then and netting out a savings figure is a little tough, but for simplicity right now, I’m assuming that by FY28, they can capture about $20M-ish in net savings between all of their initiatives from today. Interestingly, management expects to do ~14% EBITDA margins on $2.835B in sales in FY25 ($397M in EBITDA). That’s quite a bit of margin growth relative to the 11% posted today, and also note that this assumes flat organic growth, so there’s no fixed cost leverage from a sales perspective.

If I put this model together, compared to ~11.2% margins on $2.75B in annualized sales today, I’m thinking they can do 12.5% margins on FY28 sales of $2.754B. This is essentially the sum of positive pricing catching up to cost inflation and cost savings. That thus implies FY28 EBITDA of $344M. This is lower than the ~14% management is guiding for in FY25, but I’m also assuming fewer sales, so there should be fixed cost deleverage.

Valuation

From a capex perspective, I continue to model this at ~3% of sales. They’re making some additional investments today and are guiding for $90M of spend in FY25, so you could potentially argue for a higher long-term capex intensity, but I feel comfortable assuming that this continues onwards as this includes some more one-time investments and footprint realignment costs.

The same goes for the rest of their dollars. I expect them to remain focused on debt repayments following the Supreme acquisition, which I find attractive and responsible. I do worry long-term, however, about future deals down the road given the size of Supreme. It was a sizable transaction and put a lot of shareholder dollars at risk. The returns appear to be fine, so they deserve credit there, but it does add a valuation risk over time.

I’m modeling sales/EBITDA of $2.754B/$344M in FY28. Minus D&A of $96M (3.5% of sales), interest expense of $80M, tax at 25%, and CapEx of $83M (~3% of sales), I get to a FCF figure of ~$140M. This is much lower than the nearly $200M I was previously modeling, as I’m updating for weaker-than-expected Q4 results and larger future macro headwinds. And I’m also being more margin conservative.

At today’s price in $14.5/share area with 131.2M diluted shares outstanding, MBC is trading at a market cap of $1.885B. Current reported cash stands at ~$121M with reported debt of $1.008B. That debt is charged a ~8% rate, which, per their run rate EBITDA and capex, doesn’t put them in an overly dangerous position.

Zooming out then, if one believes MBC can grow beyond FY25, you’re basically looking at a 9-10% IRR even if they merely grow low-single-digit. If sales soften a little further, however, or more than expected, we’d probably be looking at ~9%. Still, this is a relatively attractive proposition - it’s merely a question of whether you think they’ll grow.

In many ways, I’ve been conservative with the assumptions I’ve made, too. For one, I’m assuming a 7% decline in sales in FY25 versus their guide for flat organic sales. That’s quite the delta for which I’m trying to account for, perhaps overoptimism on their part relative to today’s demand data points plus not-yet-in-place tariffs. And indeed, should they merely decline, say, 3%, and see additional margin leverage from growth thereafter in FY28, we’d be looking at closer to an 11% IRR. Then secondly, when it comes to margins, I’m clearly understating what are potential cost savings by modeling low-double-digit margins versus their 14%. Now, some of this is justified by different sales inputs, but some by more conservative savings assumptions.

Conclusion

All in all, I like where MBC is trading today and I am contemplating buying shares. The biggest worry I have is what’s outside of their control - i.e., the macro - and future M&A. I do believe they’ll see reduced sales in the interim from further macro degradation, but per broader recent trends of relative stability plus internal margin growth initiatives, MBC offers attractive go-forward economics that justify a nice IRR today.

data.title: BBUS: A Cost-Effective And Diversified Choice For U.S. Market Exposure
data.description:
ETF Overview

JPMorgan BetaBuilders U.S. Equity ETF (BATS:BBUS) invests in a portfolio of about 550 U.S. large-cap and mid-cap stocks that comprises about 85% of the total U.S. equity market by market capitalization. The fund has a low expense ratio of 0.02% making it a cost-efficient vehicle. BBUS’s sector allocation and fund styles are very similar to the S&P 500 index. The fund has slightly lower but largely comparable return to the S&P 500 index. However, the fund is more diversified. Together with a low expense ratio, this makes BBUS a compelling choice for investors looking to gain exposure to the broader market.

Fund Analysis

BBUS’ portfolio construction methodology favors tech sector exposure

BBUS seeks to track the Morningstar US Target Market Exposure Index. This index aims to cover approximately 85% of the U.S. equity market by market capitalization. The index is market-cap weighted. This means it has a strong tilt towards large-cap equities. Since many top stocks by market cap are technology stocks (e.g. Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia, Google, Amazon, etc.), BBUS will inevitably have a high exposure to technology sector. In fact, technology sector currently accounts for about 32.8% of BBUS’s portfolio. This makes BBUS a compelling choice as technology sector tend to outperform other sectors in the long run driven by many technological megatrends.

Comparable sector compositions to the S&P 500 index

Because BBUS covers approximately 85% of the U.S. equity market by market capitalization and the S&P 500 covers about 80%, and since both are market-cap weighted, one would expect their sector allocations to be similar. This is indeed the case. As can be seen from the sector allocation table below, both are so similar that the difference in individual sector is generally below 0.10%.

BBUS has slightly more exposure to mid-cap

In terms of equity size, since BBUS covers 85% of the U.S. equity market, while the S&P 500 covers about 80%, it is expected that BBUS will have greater allocation to smaller-cap stocks. As shown in the table below, large-cap stocks make up 81% of BBUS’s portfolio compared to 82% for the S&P 500. On the other hand, mid-cap stocks comprise approximately 19% of BBUS’s portfolio and 18% of the S&P 500. Yes, BBUS has higher exposure to smaller-cap stocks but the difference is quite small.

Now, let’s examine BBUS’ value and growth characteristics. As shown in the table below, BBUS has a slight tilt toward value, with value stocks making up approximately 27% of the portfolio, while growth stocks account for about 20%. This distribution closely mirrors the S&P 500, which has a slightly lower exposure to growth at 19%.

BBUS

Source: Morningstar (accessed on Feb. 1, 2025), created by author

BBUS has comparable performance to the S&P 500 index

Our analysis so far suggests that since both BBUS and the S&P 500 index are so similar in terms of their styles and sector allocations that both will likely have very similar returns in the future. This has indeed been the case in the past. As can be seen from the chart below, BBUS delivered a total return of 131.8% since its inception in March 2019. This return was comparable but slightly below the S&P 500 index’s return of 133.4%.

In terms of downside risk, BBUS and the S&P 500 index are both very similar, with BBUS having slightly higher downside risk. Perhaps, due to its slightly higher exposure to mid-cap and growth stocks.

BBUS is more diversified

In terms of diversification, BBUS appears to be more diversified as it includes about 550 stocks while the S&P 500 index only has 500 stocks. BBUS’s top holding makes up 7.1% of the portfolio, slightly lower than the S&P 500’s top holding at 7.4%. Additionally, BBUS’s top 10 holdings account for 34.4% of the portfolio, compared to 35.7% in the S&P 500. Therefore, BBUS is slightly more diversified.

BBUS

Source: SeekingAlpha (accessed on Feb. 26, 2025), created by author

BBUS is a cost-efficient option than most other S&P 500 index funds

BBUS currently charges an expense ratio of only 0.02%. This is very low for funds that tracks the broader market. Many S&P 500 index funds charges more than that. For example, the most famous S&P 500 fund, SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has a much higher expense ratio of 0.09%. Other notable funds such as Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) also charges a higher expense ratio of 0.03%. Therefore, BBUS appears to be a cost-efficient option for investors seeking a broader market fund.

Investor Takeaway

BBUS offers broad U.S. equity market exposure with similar sector allocation to the S&P 500 index. While its performance and downside risk are slightly higher, they are largely comparable. What stands out is BBUS’s lower expense ratio and reduced concentration risk. Therefore, we believe this is a good investment choice for investors seeking a cost-efficient way to track the performance of the broader U.S. market.

Additional Disclosure: This is not financial advice and that all financial investments carry risks. Investors are expected to seek financial advice from professionals before making any investment.

data.title: Federal Business Isn't Easy, But It Pays - Initiating Workday With A Buy
data.description:
Workday (NASDAQ:WDAY) reported strong 4Q25 results on Tuesday, causing the stock to jump 12%. The company reported $2.21 billion in revenue, up 15% year-over-year, versus $2.18 billion estimates, also beating on EPS with $1.92 per share versus $1.78 estimates. In terms of guidance, Workday expects $2.05 billion in subscription revenues in 1Q26, up 13% year-over-year but falling slightly behind the consensus of $2.06 billion, and $8.8 billion for FY26, aka 14% growth, revised up from $7.7 billion in November and in line with consensus. I’m initiating Workday with a buy for the long term, on optimism around its 1. federal business that I expect will drive growth and show on the top line, 2. Workday Student penetration and expansion, 3. management's recent efforts in cost-cutting through restructuring.

For context, Workday provides enterprises with software for finance and human resources, making its money mainly through subscriptions and professional services like training and consulting. And the best way for Workday to grow its revenue is simple: upsell its services to new (and bigger) customers and expand its services with the already existing ones. In Q4, Workday’s subscription revenues rose to $2.04B, up 15.9% year-over-year and making up over 92% of total revenue. And I think that federal customers – for example, the Department of Government Efficiency, or DOGE – are the next big thing for Workday and will drive the next leg of growth.

Hard Work Pays Off

My buy on Workday is based on three factors: First, I think the restructuring campaign will show results in FY26 and FY27. Second, the expansion of Workday Student could bring in many international customers and increase the company’s annual subscription revenue. And third, I think the federal market is fertile ground for possibly some of Workday’s biggest clients, especially after getting a foot in the door with a couple of government agencies.

Out With the Old, In With the Margins

To start off, I believe the company’s operating income has room to grow and will likely do so in FY26. On the earnings call, Workday CFO Zane Rowe talked about management’s medium-term objective for a 30% operating margin, and I think their execution is in place. This quarter, operating income was $75 million, 3.4% of revenue and down 0.7% from 4Q24 due to restructuring expenses. Not counting those, Q4 operating income would come to $584 million, or 26.4% of revenue, up 2.4% year-over-year (and not too far from that 30% either). Plus, the company slashed 8.5% of its workforce in early February to invest more heavily in AI and free up resources for international expansion. While there may be some more expenses coming up as the company makes these big investments, I think the payoff will start to show in the next year.

The March to Education

Next up: Workday Student, the company’s student information system (SIS), which streamlines things like applications, registration, and accessing records for both staff and students in colleges and universities. Plus, it leverages its in-house AI model, Workday Illuminate, to help these higher education institutions modernize their operations. Workday Student was launched in 2018 and currently has been selected by over 140 institutions in North America, nearly half of which will be live by the spring, according to CEO Carl Eschenbach. So far, with the addition of 28 institutions in CY24, there are 65 institutions live on the system, and in December, Workday announced its expansion into Australia/New Zealand. I think alongside increasing market penetration in the U.S., the expansion has strong potential to boost the adoption of Workday Student even more, which will feed into that subscription revenue that we love to see grow.

Wait, the Government Lacks Efficiency?

And now, onto my last piece of the puzzle, Workday’s federal business. Over the past year and a half, management has lasered in on federal sales more than they had historically, and I think that’s a smart move on their part because the bigger the customer, the bigger their needs, the more services they buy, and the more money for Workday and its investors. And what bigger customer could one hope for than the U.S. Federal Government, right?

If you think about it, government agencies not only bring the big checks but could possibly house the largest number of employees within the U.S. market. In 2024, the Defence Intelligence Agency, or DIA, began working with Workday to update its human resource management (HRM) systems, and now there seems to be something cooking with the DOGE (or at least the potential of it). I think that’s a great opportunity for Workday and that prioritizing efficiency, which requires modernizing government systems, could lead to higher adoption of Workday within the federal market. Eschenbach agrees with me, saying:

The systems they have, specifically ERP, HCM, or financial systems, are very antiquated…In fact, the majority of them are still on-premises, which means they’re inefficient. And as we think about DOGE and what that could potentially do going forward, if you want to drive efficiency in the government, you have to upgrade your systems.

So, given all that, I think the combination of improving operational margins, expanding Workday Student, and diving deeper into the federal market will work in Workday’s favor and pay off handsomely in the coming year and into FY27.

Valuation

Workday has a market cap of $72.11 billion and an enterprise value of $67.45 billion, with its 24-month beta of 1.21 showing some volatility in the stock. A forward P/E of 31.81, above the sector median of 23.58, suggests high investor confidence.

I won’t lie; the stock does seem expensive at this time. However, I still see growth potential, so I don’t think it’s that big of a risk, but to illustrate: The price/sales ratio of 8.28 is higher than the sector median of 3.07. Similarly, EV/sales and price/book, at 7.09 and 6.54, respectively, are well above the sector medians of 3.03 and 4.04, respectively. Interestingly, the PEG ratio, at 1.09, is below the 1.72 sector median, suggesting the stock could be somewhat underpriced and in contrast to the other metrics. Like I said, the stock does seem a bit pricey right now, but I think there’s plenty of room to grow in the near to mid-term, especially with more potential federal contracts and the tightening up of business operations.

In terms of growth, Workday’s year-over-year EPS was 2,265%, more than 200 times higher than the sector median of 11%, and its forward revenue growth is 14%, also significantly above the 6% sector median. Its profitability metrics are also looking good, with a high gross profit margin of 75.60%, outperforming the sector’s 50.15% median, and a net income margin of 20%, also far above the sector median of 4%

On revisions, Workday has had 28 upward revisions versus eight downward revisions, indicating analyst optimism. Workday’s one-year price change, down 17%, is low compared to the 4% sector median, but the stock price is up 16% over the last nine months and up 2.4% over the last three months, as shown below, slightly ahead of the S&P 500, which was flat over those three months.

What’s Next?

I think we could be seeing margin improvements in the coming year. As for what I’ll be watching, the first will certainly be any concrete developments on the federal business, plus the performance and adoption of Workday Student after the international expansion. My hopes are not too high for operating margins in the coming few quarters, considering there may be hefty expenses accompanying the restructuring, but I am definitely looking for further improvement in the non-GAAP margin as it inches towards 30% and looking forward to better margins after the expenses are dealt with, so in the midterm.

data.title: The AI Smartphone Battle Of Titans: iPhone 16 Vs. Samsung Galaxy S25
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

In the ever-evolving landscape of smartphones, Apple (AAPL) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) stand as perennial rivals, each striving to outdo the other with groundbreaking innovation. While much is published about the wonders of AI-from Nvidia Blackwell chips to sprawling data centers-these elements often feel distant, intangible. Most of us will never hold a cutting-edge AI processor in our hands or step foot inside a data center humming with computational power. Instead, our most intimate interactions with AI happen in a far more personal and accessible realm: the smartphone. These devices, carried daily in our pockets, are where the promise of AI becomes a tangible, everyday experience. And now, the stage is set for their latest masterpieces-Apple's iPhone 16 and Samsung's Galaxy S25-to redefine the possibilities of artificial intelligence.

One thing is certain: as consumers, we should be rooting for more and more competition because this can spur on different smartphone providers to produce better and better phones.

I know I wrote a number of articles during 2024 about the lead-up to Apple releasing its Apple Intelligence suite of features and ultimately the iPhone 16. It is an excellent avenue through which investors can consider a tangible example of edge AI.

A Deep Dive into the Chips: A18 Pro vs. Snapdragon 8 Elite

Even if today's premium smartphones have many, many underlying chips, at the core of the AI battle between the iPhone 16 and Galaxy S25 lies their primary respective processors-the Apple A18 Pro and the Snapdragon 8 Elite for the Galaxy. These chips are not just technical achievements, but also the engines driving the user experiences of these flagship devices. Let's break down their key attributes and how they influence AI capabilities.1

Apple A18 Pro: Precision and Privacy

Apple's A18 Pro, built on a cutting-edge 3 nanometer (nm) process, is a marvel of efficiency and design. With a focus on on-device processing, it is optimized for tasks requiring single-core performance, making it ideal for precision-driven AI functions. Apple's approach ensures that data processing occurs directly on the device, reinforcing privacy and minimizing latency.

The A18 Pro chip is equipped with advanced neural engines tailored for2:

  • Real-Time Summarization: Quickly distilling complex information into concise insights.
  • AI Photo Editing: Offering seamless background replacement, object removal and enhancement.
  • Voice Processing: Powering Siri's ability to maintain conversational context across multiple queries.

With its six-core design and integration into the iOS 18 ecosystem, the A18 Pro emphasizes refined user experiences, focusing on creative tools and productivity-enhancing features.

Snapdragon 8 Elite for Galaxy: Raw Power and Adaptability3

On the other hand, Samsung's Snapdragon 8 Elite for Galaxy processor is a powerhouse of multi-core efficiency. Also built on a 3nm process, it features an eight-core design that excels at handling complex, multitasking scenarios. This chip is built with a 40% improvement in neural processing unit (NPU) performance over its predecessor, making it a juggernaut for AI-intensive applications.

Key capabilities driven by the Snapdragon 8 Elite include:

  • Cross App Action: Seamlessly integrating tasks across multiple apps with a single command.
  • Generative Features: Advanced image manipulation, from creating visuals out of sketches to refined in-painting and out-painting capabilities.
  • ProScaler: Offering real-time upscaling for sharper visuals in both images and videos.

Samsung's focus with the Snapdragon 8 Elite is on creating an interconnected ecosystem where AI proactively anticipates and fulfills user needs. Its superior multi-core performance and enhanced NPU make it particularly suited for users seeking multitasking and advanced AI use cases.

Real-World Impact

In terms of raw performance4:

  • Single-Core Performance: Apple A18 Pro performs better compared to Snapdragon 8 Elite, making it superior for tasks requiring precision and speed.
  • Multi-Core Performance: Snapdragon 8 Elite leads over A18 Pro, highlighting its strength in multitasking and neural computations.

Now, the real difference lies in how these chips shape the AI experiences. Apple's chip enhances tools for creatives and privacy-conscious users, whereas Samsung's chip empowers multitaskers and those seeking a more dynamic, interconnected AI experience.

A Glimpse into the Apple Vision

The iPhone 16, powered by Apple's A18 Pro chip, enters the scene with a clear focus on blending productivity, creativity and user privacy. At the heart of this experience is Apple Intelligence, an on-device AI system that promises to revolutionize how users interact with their phones.

Imagine crafting the perfect email-the tone is just right, the phrasing polished-without lifting a finger. That's the power of Apple's AI-driven Writing Tools. But it doesn't stop there. Need to sift through a mountain of emails or notes? The Summarization feature condenses everything into digestible highlights, saving time and mental effort.

For the creative soul, the iPhone 16 offers a playground of possibilities. With a simple voice command, the Memories feature transforms scattered vacation snapshots into a cinematic reel. Meanwhile, Genmoji adds a personal flair, allowing users to create emojis as unique as their imaginations.

Yet, Apple's story is as much about function as it is about form. AI-powered photo editing delivers precision at your fingertips-removing unwanted objects, changing backgrounds and enhancing every detail. Pair that with Visual Intelligence, capable of identifying landmarks or summarizing a book in your hand, and the iPhone 16 becomes more than a tool; it becomes a partner.

Underlying all of this is Apple's steadfast commitment to privacy. By performing AI tasks directly on the device, Apple ensures that your data stays where it belongs-with you.

Samsung's Vision of a Smarter World

Enter Samsung's Galaxy S25, armed with the formidable Snapdragon 8 Elite for Galaxy chipset. If Apple's story is about refined elegance, Samsung's is about raw power and interconnected convenience.

Picture this: You're juggling tasks across apps, and with a single command, Cross App Action seamlessly syncs a sports schedule with your calendar. This is Samsung's promise-effortless efficiency. But Samsung doesn't stop at mere productivity. The Personalized Data Engine learns your habits, gently nudging you to enable Bluetooth when you step into your car or suggesting routines based on your day-to-day.

Samsung's AI also dives deep into the visual realm. The Sketch to Image feature takes your doodles and transforms them into vibrant artwork. Meanwhile, Generative Editing and Object Eraser give you mastery over your photos, whether it's removing shadows or refining fine details. And for content creators, the ProScaler promises real-time image upscaling, ensuring every shot looks as sharp as possible.

But perhaps the Galaxy S25's most intriguing trait is its ability to anticipate your needs. From contextual insights via Now Brief to intelligent audio management with Audio Eraser, Samsung's AI feels almost alive. It's not just assisting-it's proactively engaging.

The Clash of Philosophies

The iPhone 16 and Galaxy S25 don't just differ in features; they embody contrasting philosophies. Apple champions privacy and on-device processing, catering to users who demand control over their data and a seamless creative ecosystem. Samsung, on the other hand, emphasizes interconnectedness and personalization, crafting a device that feels like a true companion in every sense.

This divergence extends to performance. Apple's A18 Pro chip delivers stellar single-core performance, ideal for tasks requiring precision and speed. Samsung's Snapdragon 8 Elite for Galaxy, with its superior multi-core and neural processing power, thrives in multitasking and intensive AI applications. Whether it's crafting an emoji or editing a high-resolution video, these devices cater to vastly different needs.

Choosing Your Champion

Ultimately, the choice between the iPhone 16 and Galaxy S25 boils down to priorities. Do you value privacy and a polished, cohesive experience? Or does the allure of an AI companion, tailored to your habits and integrated across every aspect of your digital life, resonate more?

In this battle of titans, there is no definitive winner-only a reflection of what you, the user, value most. One thing is certain: both Apple and Samsung are pushing the boundaries of what AI can achieve, ensuring that the real victor is innovation itself. The real journey we find ourselves on is from AI as a novelty feature on the newest, most premium phones to a world, coming soon, where AI is just a component in the core operating system of almost every phone.

AI at Your Fingertips: Investing in the Smartphone Revolution

For investors looking to tap into this transformative megatrend, the WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence and Innovation Fund (WTAI) offers a compelling opportunity. By investing in a diversified portfolio of companies driving AI innovation, WTAI provides exposure to the cutting-edge technologies shaping the future of industries like mobile, healthcare and beyond. As AI evolves, funds like WTAI position investors to benefit from the ongoing AI revolution.

Footnotes

1 Source: Michael Acton, "Samsung Unveils New AI-Powered Smartphone in Fight against Apple," Financial Times, 1/23/25.

2 Source: Apple introduces iPhone 16 and iPhone 16 Plus

3 Source: Snapdragon 8 Elite Mobile Platform

4 Source: Jessie Hollington, "Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Elite vs. Apple A18 Pro: Two Top-Tier Chips Clash," Digital Trends, 11/23/24.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. The Fund invests in companies primarily involved in the investment theme of Artificial Intelligence and Innovation. Companies engaged in AI typically face intense competition and potentially rapid product obsolescence. These companies are also heavily dependent on intellectual property rights and may be adversely affected by loss or impairment of those rights. Additionally, AI companies typically invest significant amounts of spending on research and development, and there is no guarantee that the products or services produced by these companies will be successful. Companies that are capitalizing on Innovation and developing technologies to displace older technologies or create new markets may not be successful. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets. The composition of the Index is governed by an Index Committee and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund's prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund's risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree's London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU's Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor's degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Original Post

data.title: Rolls-Royce: A Strong 2024 And More Still To Come (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEF) issues its full-year results this week and excited investors both with its performance and upgraded future performance expectations. That propelled its share price to a new all-time high, with the stock (already one of the FTSE 100’s best performers last year, after topping its leaderboard the prior year) up 20%.

I last covered the name in November 2023 with my hold piece “Rolls-Royce: Ongoing Performance Recovery”, since when it has soared 163%.

Here’s an update based on the full-year results.

2024 Results came in Strongly

Without doubt, last year was a strong one for the company. Revenue, underlying profit and underlying free cash flow all grew significantly.

Of those various performance metrics, the only one that catches my eye as a potential watchout is statutory pre-tax profit. While it surged 81% on an underlying basis, it actually fell back on a statutory basis.

A look at the income statement shows what’s going on here.

Financing costs are the key driver. The prior year, Rolls had financing income of £482m. This time around, it paid financing costs of £688m.

That does not particularly concern me given the positive improvement in Rolls’ balance sheet.

Rolls-Royce has Transformed its Balance Sheet

Rolls ended the prior year with net debt of close to £2bn. It ended last year with net cash of £475m.

For a company that was on its knees and borrowed heavily during the pandemic era of weak civil aviation demand (in 2021 net debt was above £5bn), that is a superb turnaround.

Current management has a sharp focus on cash flows and balance sheet management and I think this is becoming increasingly clear. Indeed, it is so confident of the current position that it announced a £1bn share buyback to start immediately, for completion this year.

Underlying free cash flow is strong.

The company expects even stronger performance this year, of £2.7bn-2.9bn. That was its 2027 target before, so it has brought it forward two years.

The Dividend has Been Reinstated

The company brought back its dividend, at 6p per share for 2024. That is based on a 30% payout ratio of underlying profit after tax (personally I think it could be a lot more aggressive than this).

Like the buyback, I see the dividend reintroduction as a significant indicator of management confidence. If business performance continues strongly, I expect it to grow in coming years.

Rolls-Royce has Upgraded its Medium-Term Targets

The share surge following the results announcement was, I think, largely driven by the fact that not only did Rolls unveil a strong 2024 performance (as had been expected), it also set out more bullish medium-term targets.

Previously, at a capital markets day (CMD), it had set out 2027 targets. In its results, the date was a year later (2028) partly because Rolls has already met some of the 2027 targets. The new, 2028 ones, are more ambitious.

Rolls has the wind in its sails so this makes sense to me. European defence spending is ramping up, civil aviation has recovered well from the pandemic and demand for power systems remains in growth mode. Indeed, last year saw double digit organic growth in underlying revenue across all three divisions.

With demand high and the company taking a disciplined approach to business delivery and spending that has already transformed it, I think the 2028 targets are a welcome boost for the investment case.

That said, there are some risks here. The key one I see is a sudden, unexpected sustained downturn in demand for civil aviation. That could hurt both sales and servicing for the engines business, which thanks to high fixed costs struggles to absorb such sudden downturns without significant negative impact on the bottom line (as we saw in the pandemic).

History tells us that a non-sudden cyclical downturn in demand (e.g. due to a very weak economy caused by global recession) can also hurt both revenues and profits in the civil aviation division.

The aggressive business approach taken by Rolls over the past several years has certainly delivered results, but it also brings a risk in my view. Cost-cutting is a tricky business to get right, especially in an industry like aeronautical engineering, where the full impacts may not be clear for decades (as we have seen at Boeing (BA)). Although I think the performance looks promising, it remains to be seen whether Rolls-Royce’s transformation is sustainable. Historically the business has been an inconsistent performer.

The Shares Potentially Look Like Good Value

Given the updated targets, the shares trade on around 15 times projected 2028 free cash flow.

That does not look expensive to me.

Still, personally I do not like the risk profile as we have seen the business struggle badly in the past with sudden demand slumps outside its control and I think that could happen again.

Still, the valuation is attractive so on that basis I think it makes sense for me to upgrade my rating to “buy”.

data.title: NewAmsterdam Pharma: Significant Upside Possible In Case Of Approval Of Obicetrapib
data.description:
Thesis

NewAmsterdam Pharma (NASDAQ:NAMS) is a biotech company with one lead asset, namely obicetrapib which the company aims to develop for a number of indications related to cardiovascular risk. obicetrapib is a potentially best-in-class second-generation CETP inhibitor. It aims at lowering LDL-C, the bad cholesterol, in combination with available therapies aimed at lowering lipids such as statins. The goal of therapy is to lower LDL-C in patients whose LDL-C is not adequately controlled despite being on maximally tolerated lipid-lowering therapy.

The company has recently reported Phase 3 results from three major trials: BROADWAY, TANDEM and BROOKLYN. These trials were successful, meeting their primary endpoint with statistical significance. That means the company can now file for approval, which it plans to in the second half of 2025 in Europe first. The company intends to release further data in the coming months, and has three further trials ongoing: PREVAIL, VINCENT and REMBRANDT. The PREVAIL trial is the most interesting one, because it could confirm cardiovascular benefit.

There is competition in the lipid-lowering space, especially from PCSK9 inhibitors, but these are expensive and injectable which leads to poor patient compliance. obicetrapib is an oral drug candidate.

The markets in which obicetrapib can make a difference are huge, addressing potentially up to 30 million people.

The company is currently trading at a market cap of $2 billion, after a significant retracement over the past months. After an upsized financing of $479 million in December 2024, the company had $834.2 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities, which I believe should be sufficient to launch commercial operations.

My valuation exercise indicates to me that there is significant potential upside, as a market value of in between $6.78 billion and $21.69 billion may be possible in the case of approval and successful commercialization.

Company

Introduction

Over the course of 2025, NewAmsterdam Pharma has reported on three Phase 3 trials with obicetrapib. These three trials are part of its package to gain approval. The company has three further Phase 3 trial ongoing.

Obicetrapib is a second-generation CETP inhibitor, which aims to lower the bad LDL-C cholesterol and raise the good LDL-H cholesterol. It comes as an oral small molecule drug without negative effects on blood pressure, fat accumulation and with minimal drug-drug interactions. It thereby differentiates itself from other CETP inhibitors which have been tested, such as Pfizer’s (PFE) torcetrapib which led to less LDL-C reduction but came with increase in blood pressure and aldosterone, Merck’s (MRK) anacetrapib which came with fat accumulation issues and had lower efficacy.

The unmet need lies in the fact that, even with statins and PCSK9 inhibitors already approved, many patients fail to achieve their target LDL cholesterol levels. Obicetrapib can make a difference for these patients, as a drug to be taken much easier than PCSK9 inhibitors that does not come with any side effects. This slide of the company’s corporate presentation shows where they see obicetrapib fitting into the treatment landscape.

I will now review the reported and upcoming Phase 3 trials. This is the trial design for the three trials that have been reported.

The BROADWAY trial

The Phase 3 BROADWAY trial assessed obicetrapib in 2,530 patients with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease [ASCVD] and/or heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia [HeFH], with too high LDL-C despite being on lipid-lowering therapy.

NAMS reported topline data of this trial in December of last year. obicetrapib showed a 33% reduction of LDL-C at day 84.

Importantly, here, the drug also showed a 21% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events at one year, although reduction of MACE was not the trial’s primary endpoint. This derisks the PREVAIL trial discussed below considerably.

The TANDEM trial

The Phase 3 TANDEM trial tried obicetrapib in 407 patients with ASCVD and/or HeFH, in combination with ezetimibe. The company reported on that trial in November 2024. obicetrapib and ezetimibe combined showed a 50% reduction of LDL-C, with more than 70% achieving levels below 55 mg/dl. That combination led to a 48.6% reduction in LDL-C levels, which is in line with PCSK9 inhibitors.

The BROOKLYN trial

The Phase 3 BROOKLYN trial tested obicetrapib in 354 patients with HeFH, with too high LDL-C despite being on lipid-lowering therapy, for one year. obicetrapib showed LDL-C reductions of 36% over the course of treatment, at day 84. In November 2024, the company presented additional data showing that LDL-C had been reduced to 41.5% at the one year timepoint. Lp(a) had been further reduced from 45.9% at day 84 to 54.3% at day 365.

Takeaways

The takeaway from the above trials is that obicetrapib systematically lowers LDL-C in combination with existing therapies with about 33-45%, and also reduces Lp(a) levels with about 40-60% which is more than the competition such as inclisiran and bempedoic acid.

The LDL-reduction seen with obicetrapib therapy is similar across therapies, and continues over a longer timeframe.

This puts obicetrapib, whether or not in combination with ezetimibe, in a good spot to replace PCSK9 inhibitors. In combination with PCSK9 inhibitors, their efficacy is identical, and obicetrapib lacks the side effects and difficult treatment profile that are typical to PCSK9 inhibitors.

Ongoing Trials

The PREVAIL trial (Phase 3)

The PREVAIL test will test obicetrapib in 9,500 patients with a history of ASCVD. Enrollment was complete in April 2024, and the trial should report topline data in 2026. The trial’s primary endpoint is major adverse cardiovascular events reduction [MACE], which of course is the essence of any therapy that focus on cardiovascular risk reduction. The expectation is that obicetrapib will be able to lead to a reduction of 15% or more. If so, the drug candidate would outperform other available therapies. Bempedoic acid leads to a MACE reduction of 13%, while PCSK9 inhibitors lead to a 15% MACE reduction.

The VINCENT trial (Phase 2)

The Phase 2 VINCENT trial will test obicetrapib alone and in combination with evolocumab on Lp(a) in patients with mild dyslipidemia. This is a single arm study in two cohorts, split in two periods of 8 weeks. The cohorts are differentiated based on their levels of Lp(a). During the first period, patients will receive obicetrapib solely. During the next 8 weeks, evolocumab will be added to the regimen.

The REMBRANDT trial (Phase 3)

The REMBRANDT trial will test obicetrapib plus ezetimibe in patients with high-risk ASCVD with evidence of coronary plaque who are not adequately controlled by their maximally tolerated lipid-modifying therapy. This is a smaller study, enrolling only 300 patients.

Competitive field

There are several other players in the lipid-lowering space, such as PCSK9 inhibitors, bempedoic acid, or ezetimibe. Of those, Amgen’s (AMGN) Repatha, Sanofi’s (SNY) Praluent and Novartis’ (NVS) Leqvio are the most efficacious, but they are expensive and need to be injected, which leads to poor patient compliance. Bempedoic acid is an oral therapy, but only leads to about 20% LDL-C reduction, similar to ezetimibe.

The potential competitive pipeline is also rather limited.

Next steps

The company just announced that it would file for approval in Europe in the second half of 2025. I assume the US will follow shortly after. The requests for approval would be for an LDL-C lowering therapy based on the three above-mentioned Phase 3 trials.

In 2025, further data from the three finalized Phase 3 trials should be reported, as well as data from the VINCENT trial.

A filing for approval for MACE could only follow some time in 2027, once results from the PREVAIL trial have been reported. That trial should normally have data some time in 2026, as should the REMBRANDT trial.

Valuation

As the company gets ready for commercialization, it is important to assess the markets it can be active in, and the playing field of the competition.

Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death in the world, taking 17.9 million deaths each year, and despite available therapies, it remains on the rise. The goal of lipid-lowering therapies is to prevent that. The market for cholesterol-lowering therapies is huge. The global lipid-lowering drug market is valued at $35 billion in 2025 and should grow to $48 billion by 2034.

If I were to assume drug pricing of $5,000 per year, similar to PCSK9 inhibitors, and peak sales at 2029, then 250,000 patients per year would lead to annual sales of $1.25 billion. A peak sales assumption of 800,000 patients per year would lead to $4 billion in sales per year. Assuming an EV/revenue multiple of 5, which I believe is lower than a 6.79 multiple typical to the sector, that would lead to a market valuation of in between $6 and $20 billion per year. From that perspective, I believe NAMS is currently undervalued. Margins in the pharmaceutical sector are often as high as 50% or more. Applying a discount rate of 10% which is standard to the biotech sector, and a terminal growth rate of 2%, a market value of $6.78 billion up to $21.69 billion could be assumed. Those market valuations would translate to about to a share price of $66.8 to $213. From that perspective, I believe that at a market cap of $2 billion and a share price of 19.7, NAMS still has significant upside potential.

Financials

NAMS has just reported full year 2024 results. After an upsized financing of $479 million that took place in December 2024, the company had $834.2 in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities at the end of 2024. That leaves the company with the necessary capital to start the commercial launch of obicetrapib.

Revenue for the year 2024 was $45.6, mostly related to milestone payments. Net loss was $241.6 million, which was higher than $176.9 million in 2023, and much higher than 2022. That still means NAMS has plenty of cash to start commercial operations in case of approval. In case of a successful launch, further financing may not be required, but of course a further financing always remains possible if such launch proves to be slower than anticipated.

Risks

The biggest risk for the company right now, in my eyes, is regulatory. If, for some reason, the regulatory authorities decline a request for approval or request additional trials to be run, that would mean a considerable setback for the company. I consider such risk low at this time.

There is also quite some competition in the lipid-lowering space, so one is unsure how the future of the competitive landscape may look like.

Once approved, NAMS will still have to operate a commercial team to ramp up sales. At this point, there is no certainty that commercialization will be successful, and that market adoption will be in line with investor expectations.

Finally, the ongoing clinical trials could fail, leading to negative investor sentiment.

Conclusion

NAMS is a biotech company that inches closer and closer to approval and commercialization of its lead drug obicetrapib in a number of cardiovascular indications. Obicetrapib is not a standalone therapy, it has to be combined with existing approved lipid-lowering drugs, in patients whose LDL-C is still too high.

Obicetrapib has proven successful in a number of Phase 3 trials, and the company will now file for approval before the European Medicines Agency in the second half of 2025. I consider the chances for approval high. In case of approval, the next step is commercialization.

The lipid-lowering market is huge, and I believe obicetrapib can differentiate itself and find a space in the above-mentioned competitive space.

My valuation model shows me that the stock may have significant upside in case of approval, chances of which I consider highly likely.

NAMS is therefore a buy for me.

data.title: BUZZ Investing: Markets Navigate Policy Shifts
data.description:
Financial markets grappled with a storm of political maneuvering around trade, taxes, and regulation, stoking some investor unease.

During the recent period between index selection dates (January 8, 2025 - February 12, 2025, the "Period"), financial markets were shaped by political developments, central bank policy, and significant disruption in the technology sector. President Donald Trump's inauguration marked the beginning of an active policy agenda, with a series of executive orders and legislative initiatives focused on trade, corporate taxation, tariffs, and regulatory changes. While these actions provided some clarity on economic priorities, investors continued to assess their broader implications. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve, in its January 29 meeting, maintained its benchmark interest rate at 4.25%-4.50%, emphasizing a patient, data-driven approach. Fed Chair Jerome Powell reinforced the central bank's commitment to inflation management while acknowledging risks to economic growth, setting the stage for potential policy friction with the new administration. Market volatility remained elevated as investors weighed these factors alongside inflation concerns and the evolving interest rate outlook.

A defining event in the technology sector was the emergence of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), a Chinese artificial intelligence firm, which introduced a suite of large language models (LLMs) that rivaled those of leading U.S. companies at significantly lower costs. The announcement triggered a sharp repricing in the sector, with Nvidia declining 17% and the Nasdaq Composite falling nearly 3% in a single session as investors reassessed AI leadership and competitive pressures. Meanwhile, geopolitical tensions, particularly in Ukraine, drove gold prices to record highs, reinforcing a risk-off sentiment in certain market segments. The "Magnificent Seven" tech stocks struggled, with most underperforming except for Meta, which surged nearly 19% during the Period. Despite challenges in the tech sector, the S&P 500 remained near its all-time high, buoyed by resilience in other sectors. While the Nasdaq initially suffered from AI-related disruptions, both it and the broader equity indices finished the Period higher, supported by international market strength and ongoing confidence in the economic outlook.

The BUZZ NextGen AI US Sentiment Leaders Index ("BUZZ Index") returned 2.66% during the month of January compared to a return of 2.78% for the S&P 500 Index during the same period.

Shares of Palantir Technologies pace BUZZ Index Gains

Palantir Technologies' (PLTR) stock surged 72% during the recent period, driven by strong earnings, upbeat guidance, and growing demand for its AI and defense analytics solutions. The company's results exceeded expectations, highlighting robust commercial growth and reinforcing confidence in its long-term trajectory. Unlike AI hardware firms that faced volatility after China's DeepSeek AI announcement, Palantir remained resilient. While DeepSeek raised concerns about AI commoditization, Palantir's pricing model and focus on operational AI insulated it from price pressures. The company positioned itself as a strategic AI partner, emphasizing integration and decision-making over raw model capabilities. Palantir's deep government ties, particularly its work with the Department of Defense, also bolstered investor optimism amid rising federal defense spending. Wall Street analysts raised price targets, citing its strong positioning in AI-driven decision-making. Broader enthusiasm for AI, including the Oracle-OpenAI-SoftBank-backed 'Stargate' initiative, further fueled momentum, solidifying Palantir's role as a leader in applied AI.

Shares of Trump Media & Technology among declining stocks in the BUZZ Index

Shares of Trump Media & Technology Group (DJT) fell during the recent Period, dropping nearly 12% as post-election enthusiasm waned and investor concerns over fundamentals resurfaced. The stock had surged following Trump's victory, driven by speculative buying, but the rally lost steam after his inauguration as investors took profits amid renewed skepticism about the company's long-term viability. Adding to the pressure, Trump's launch of the $TRUMP cryptocurrency token raised ethical concerns and potential regulatory scrutiny. The token's 48% decline over the past week further dampened confidence, fueling uncertainty around its implications for Trump's broader business ventures. While some investors see long-term potential in Trump's continued use of Truth Social as a direct communication platform, DJT's stock remains highly volatile. Its future will likely be shaped by political developments, regulatory factors, and market confidence in the company's ability to sustain growth beyond Trump's presidency.

BUZZ Index February 2025 Rebalance Highlights

Hims & Hers Health Inc.

The rise of telehealth has transformed the healthcare industry, with companies rapidly adopting digital-first solutions to meet growing consumer demand. Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) has been at the forefront of this shift, leveraging its direct-to-consumer model to expand access to personalized treatments for weight management, mental health, hair regrowth, and sexual wellness. The company has particularly benefited from the growing acceptance of prescription weight-loss medications, fueled by the widespread popularity of GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic. Investor enthusiasm for HIMS has surged alongside its business momentum, with shares climbing more than fivefold since 2024, including doubling in the first two months of this year. The company further capitalized on its visibility with a high-profile Super Bowl commercial, which drove a spike in consumer engagement. This rising traction has also translated into increased recognition among online investors, with HIMS' weighting in the BUZZ Index climbing from 1.57% in January 2025 to 2.79% this month, reflecting its growing influence in the telehealth space.

IonQ Inc.

Since the turn of the millennium, the world has experienced two major technological revolutions: the explosion of the internet and the rise of artificial intelligence. With AI reaching mainstream adoption just two years ago following the launch of ChatGPT, the next breakthrough may already be taking shape-this time in quantum computing. For decades, quantum mechanics was purely theoretical, but recent scientific advancements have brought practical applications closer to reality. If fully realized, quantum computers could process information millions of times faster than today's most advanced supercomputers. While widespread adoption remains years away, groundbreaking progress by Google and NASA has fueled a surge of interest in the space, driving investor enthusiasm toward companies like IonQ, Inc. (IONQ). Founded in 2015, IonQ went public in 2021 through a SPAC merger with dMY Technology Group III. After an initial wave of excitement, the stock struggled to gain momentum, with interest fading as quantum computing remained in its early stages. However, recent breakthroughs have reignited enthusiasm, propelling shares more than 16 times higher from their post-merger lows. This month, IonQ ranks within the top 25 names in the BUZZ Index, holding a 1.47% weight, as investor confidence in quantum computing continues to grow.
